concept_name: Projectile Down an Incline — Slope Helps the Projectile Go Further
source_book: DC Pandey 7.7 — projectile on an inclined plane (down); split from projectile_inclined

real_world_anchor.primary: A mountain cyclist at Manali launching off a 12-degree downhill switchback ramp at 15 m/s at 20 degrees above horizontal. Unlike up-slope shots, here the ground FALLS AWAY — so the projectile stays airborne longer and lands much further than it would on flat ground. This is why ski jumpers and BMX riders score well on downhill ramps; the slope multiplies their range.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A water-jet fountain at Ramoji Film City angled downhill to spray over a cascading garden slope of 25 degrees. Water leaves the nozzle at 8 m/s, 15 degrees above horizontal. Down the slope, the jets reach much further than the same jets fired over flat ground — dramatic for visitors to watch.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A basketball bounce pass from the top of a Dalhousie hill school's sports terrace, landing on a lower playground 8 metres below and 30 m away. The ball arcs downhill. Teachers unconsciously use projectile-down-incline physics when coaching young players to 'aim low across the slope'.

formulas.range_down: R_down = 2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha + beta) * cos(alpha) / (g * cos^2(beta))
formulas.optimal_angle_down: For max range down an incline: alpha_opt = 45 deg - beta / 2  (bisector of incline and vertical)
formulas.rotated_axes_approach: Rotate axes so x-axis lies ALONG the incline (pointing downslope). The perpendicular becomes 'vertical'. Gravity splits: g*sin(beta) along the slope (ACCELERATING downslope motion), g*cos(beta) perpendicular.

constraints: Launch and landing both ON the incline (same slope).
Down-slope always gives LARGER range than flat ground for the same alpha.
Slope adds downward assist to the flight.
At beta = 0, formula reduces to flat-ground case.
Beta must be positive (slope going down).

STATE_1.title: Down-Slope Gives the Projectile More Air Time

scene_composition.text:
Manali downhill switchback, 12 deg slope down:

   cyclist launches at 15 m/s, alpha = 20 deg above horizontal

      .__________________
       .                  LAUNCH POINT
        .
         .
           .  beta = 12 deg
             .
               .        (ground drops away)
                 .
                   .
                     .__________ LANDING SPOT

   Unlike flat ground, the ground falls AWAY beneath the ball.
   Ball stays in the air LONGER -> travels FURTHER.

   Compared to the same launch on flat ground,
   down-slope shots can go 1.5x to 3x the flat-ground range.
Manali mountain cyclist over a downhill ramp

tts_sentences.text_en: On a Manali downhill switchback, a mountain cyclist launches off a 12-degree ramp at 15 metres per second, 20 degrees above horizontal.
Unlike flat ground, the ground FALLS AWAY beneath the cyclist. The bike stays airborne longer — and lands much further down the slope.
Down-slope shots routinely go 1.5 to 3 times the flat-ground range, for the same launch speed and angle. The slope gifts you distance.

STATE_2.title: Rotated Axes — Gravity ACCELERATES Motion Along the Slope

scene_composition.text:
Rotate axes downhill (x' points downslope):

   gravity g (straight down) splits into:
      g_x'  =  g * sin(beta)      (along-slope, ACCELERATING downslope motion!)
      g_y'  =  g * cos(beta)      (perpendicular, pulling ball to slope)

   Key difference from UP-slope:
      up-slope: g_x' ACTS AGAINST motion -> DECELERATES
      down-slope: g_x' IS IN DIRECTION OF motion -> ACCELERATES

   In rotated launch velocity:
      v_x'  =  v0 * cos(alpha + beta)   (along downslope direction)
      v_y'  =  v0 * sin(alpha + beta)   (perpendicular above slope)

   Notice the '+ beta' instead of '- beta'.
   The effective launch angle above the slope is LARGER
   because the slope is below the horizontal.
Rotated Axes — Gravity ACCELERATES Motion Along the Slope
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Rotate the axes downhill, so x-prime points along the slope.
Gravity splits into g sine beta along the slope, PLUS g cosine beta perpendicular.
Crucial difference from up-slope — here g sine beta accelerates motion DOWN the slope, rather than decelerating it.
The rotated launch angle above the slope is alpha plus beta, not alpha minus beta. The effective launch angle is LARGER because the slope tilts the reference.

STATE_3.title: Range Formula and Optimal Angle Down-Slope

scene_composition.text:
Range formula down an incline:

   R  =  2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha + beta) * cos(alpha)  /  (g * cos^2(beta))

  Note the '+ beta' vs the up-slope '- beta'.
  That sign flip is what makes down-slope longer.

  Time of flight:
    T  =  2 * v0 * sin(alpha + beta) / (g * cos(beta))

  Optimal angle for maximum down-slope range:
    alpha_opt  =  45 deg  -  beta / 2   (shallower than flat ground!)

  Intuition: because the slope already helps, you should aim
             SHALLOWER, letting the downslope do the work.

  Manali numbers (v0 = 15, alpha = 20, beta = 12):
    sin(32)    =  0.530
    cos(20)    =  0.940
    cos^2(12)  ~=  0.958

    R  =  2 * 225 * 0.530 * 0.940 / (10 * 0.958)
       ~=  23.4 m along the slope
Range Formula and Optimal Angle Down-Slope
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Range down an incline is 2 v-nought squared sine of alpha plus beta, times cosine alpha, all divided by g cosine-squared beta.
The sign flip from minus to plus beta is what makes down-slope go further.
Optimal angle for maximum down-slope range is 45 degrees MINUS beta over 2 — shallower than flat ground.
The intuition — because the slope already helps, you should aim shallower and let the downslope do the work.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student uses the up-slope formula with the same '-' sign for down-slope, getting a nonsense answer.
confusion_patterns: same formula up and down slope
confuse up and down incline formula
sign of beta for down slope
how is down slope different from up slope

scene_composition.text:
Student's mistake:

  Up-slope formula (alpha - beta):
    R_up = 2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha - beta) * cos(alpha) / (g * cos^2(beta))

  Down-slope formula (alpha + beta):
    R_down = 2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha + beta) * cos(alpha) / (g * cos^2(beta))

  Student applies the up-slope formula to a down-slope problem
  -> gets a SMALLER range than flat ground
  -> obviously wrong (down-slope should be larger)

  The sign of beta in the sine term DEPENDS ON whether
  the slope rises or falls in front of the launch point.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: The common mistake — using the wrong sign for beta.
Up-slope uses alpha minus beta. Down-slope uses alpha plus beta.
Apply up-slope to a down-slope problem, and you will get a smaller range than flat ground — obviously wrong since down-slope should be LARGER.
The sign of beta depends on whether the slope rises or falls AWAY from the launch point.

STATE_2.title: Which Sign? Look at the Slope Direction

scene_composition.text:
Sign rule:

   UP-SLOPE (slope RISES away from launch):
       Effective angle above slope is SMALLER
       -> use  sin(alpha - beta)
       -> shorter range

   DOWN-SLOPE (slope FALLS away from launch):
       Effective angle above slope is LARGER
       -> use  sin(alpha + beta)
       -> longer range

  Memory trick:
    'Up takes away (minus), down gives (plus).'

  Picture the slope. If it goes UP, subtract. If DOWN, add.
Which Sign? Look at the Slope Direction
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Sign rule to burn into memory.
Up-slope — slope rises away from the launch — use sine of alpha minus beta. Effective launch angle above slope is smaller. Range is shorter.
Down-slope — slope falls away from the launch — use sine of alpha plus beta. Effective angle is larger, range is longer.
Memory trick — 'Up takes away. Down gives.' Minus for up, plus for down.

scene_composition.text:
Both formulas must reduce to flat-ground at beta = 0:

  Up-slope formula:
    R  =  2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha - 0) * cos(alpha) / (g * cos^2(0))
       =  2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha) * cos(alpha) / g
       =  v0^2 * sin(2*alpha) / g  <- classic flat-ground range. OK.

  Down-slope formula:
    R  =  2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha + 0) * cos(alpha) / (g * cos^2(0))
       =  2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha) * cos(alpha) / g
       =  v0^2 * sin(2*alpha) / g  <- also flat-ground range. OK.

  Both formulas behave correctly in the flat-ground limit.
  The ONLY difference is the sign of beta in the sin term.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Sanity check — both formulas must reduce to the flat-ground formula when beta equals zero.
Up-slope formula at beta equals zero — v-nought squared sine 2 alpha over g. Correct.
Down-slope formula at beta equals zero — same result, v-nought squared sine 2 alpha over g. Also correct.
So the only real difference between the two formulas is the sign of beta inside the sine term. Plus for down, minus for up.
